questions.question: Explain the linked representation of binary trees with a neat diagram. Also mention the fields in each node.
questions.hint: Draw a node structure showing data, left and right child pointers
keyPoints: Each node contains three fields: data, left pointer, right pointer
Left pointer stores address of left child
Right pointer stores address of right child
NULL indicates absence of child
Diagram showing node structure with arrow pointers
Root pointer stores address of first node

questions.question: Explain the sequential (array) representation of binary trees. How is a complete binary tree stored efficiently?
questions.hint: Remember the formulas: left child = 2i+1, right child = 2i+2, parent = (i-1)/2
keyPoints: Array index 0 stores root node
For any node at index i, left child at 2i+1
For any node at index i, right child at 2i+2
Parent of node at index i is at (i-1)/2
Complete binary tree uses minimum space with no gaps
NULL or special value for missing nodes

questions.question: Compare linked representation and sequential representation of binary trees based on (i) space efficiency and (ii) ease of traversal.
questions.hint: Consider worst case (skewed tree) vs best case (complete tree) for both
keyPoints: Linked: uses 3n pointers for n nodes, may have overhead
Sequential: for complete trees uses exactly n locations
Linked: flexible for sparse/incomplete trees
Sequential: simple index calculations for traversal
Linked: requires pointer manipulation
Sequential: direct access using index formula

questions.question: What is a complete binary tree? How is it represented in memory using an array with an example?
questions.hint: Draw a complete binary tree with 7 nodes and show corresponding array
keyPoints: Complete binary tree: all levels filled except possibly last
Last level has nodes as left as possible
Array representation has no gaps
Root at index 0
Example tree with 7 nodes showing array indices
No NULL entries needed in complete portion

questions.question: For a binary tree stored in an array, if a node is stored at index 7, find the indices of its parent, left child, and right child.
keyPoints: Parent index = (7-1)/2 = 3
Left child index = 2*7 + 1 = 15
Right child index = 2*7 + 2 = 16
Verify parent-child relationship
Show formula application

questions.question: Consider the following array representation of a binary tree: Index: 0 A, 1 B, 2 C, 3 D, 4 E, 5 NULL, 6 F. Draw the binary tree structure represented by this array.
questions.hint: Build tree level by level using parent-child index formulas
keyPoints: A at index 0 is root
B at index 1 is left child of A
C at index 2 is right child of A
D at index 3 is left child of B
E at index 4 is right child of B
F at index 6 is right child of C
NULL at index 5 means no right child of C's left
Tree diagram with correct parent-child relationships

questions.question: Draw a binary tree with at least 8 nodes and show its representation using (i) linked representation and (ii) sequential representation. Compare space requirements.
questions.hint: Use 4 bytes for pointer and data fields for space calculation
keyPoints: Draw original binary tree with labeled nodes
Linked: Show node structures with data, left, right pointers
Show NULL for missing children
Array: Show array with indices 0 to n-1
Count pointers in linked representation
Count array locations used
Calculate total space in bytes (assuming known sizes)
Comparison showing linked needs extra pointers

questions.question: Explain the algorithm to insert a new node as the left child of a given node in the linked representation of a binary tree. Also explain the challenges in array representation for the same operation.
questions.hint: Consider all cases including when node already has left child
keyPoints: Algorithm steps: create new node, find target node, update pointers
Allocate memory for new node
Set new node's data
Set new node's left to target's left
Set target's left to new node
Handle case when target already has left child
Array: may require shifting elements
Array: may run out of space, need resizing

questions.question: A binary tree has 15 nodes arranged as a complete binary tree. Calculate the memory required to store it using (i) linked representation (assuming int=2bytes, pointer=2bytes) and (ii) array representation (int=2bytes).
questions.hint: In linked rep, each node has 2 pointers regardless of having children or not
keyPoints: Linked: each node needs 2(int) + 2(pointer) + 2(pointer) = 6 bytes
For 15 nodes: 15 × 6 = 90 bytes
Array: needs 15 integers = 15 × 2 = 30 bytes
Array representation uses less memory
Difference: 60 bytes saved
Linked has overhead of 30 pointers (NULL)

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of linked representation over sequential representation for binary trees. When would you prefer one over the other?
questions.hint: Think about use cases like heaps vs general binary trees
keyPoints: Linked advantages: dynamic size, no memory wasted, easy insertion/deletion
Linked disadvantages: extra memory for pointers, no random access, complex traversal
Sequential advantages: no pointer overhead, fast random access, simple traversal
Sequential disadvantages: may waste space for sparse trees, fixed size issues
Prefer linked: for sparse trees, frequent modifications
Prefer sequential: complete/similar to complete, heaps, priority queues

questions.question: In array representation of a binary tree, if the root is at index 0 and left child is at index 4, what is the index of its parent? Also state the relationship used.
questions.hint: Use reverse of left child formula: i = 2*parent + 1
keyPoints: Parent index = (left child index - 1) / 2
For any node at index i: parent = floor((i-1)/2)
Formula derivation from 2i+1 = left child index
Verify by checking if 1's left child gives 4

questions.question: Given a binary tree with 10 nodes stored in an array starting at index 0, answer the following: (a) What is the maximum possible index? (b) If index 4 has no children, which indices are empty in the array? (c) What is the parent of index 9?
questions.hint: Consider that indices may exist but contain no valid nodes
keyPoints: (a) Maximum index = 10 - 1 = 9
(b) Node at index 4: left child would be at 9, right at 10 (doesn't exist)
Since n=10, indices 10 and beyond are empty/not used
(c) Parent of 9 = floor((9-1)/2) = 4
Verify: left child of 4 would be at 2*4+1=9

questions.question: Design an algorithm to delete a node from a binary tree represented using linked representation. Consider all possible cases: leaf node, node with one child, node with two children.
questions.hint: Two children case is complex - consider using inorder successor
keyPoints: Case 1 - Leaf node: simply free the node, set parent's pointer to NULL
Case 2 - One child: replace node's data with child's data, adjust pointers
Case 3 - Two children: find inorder successor (or predecessor)
Replace node's value with successor's value
Delete successor node (which is leaf or has one child)
Handle root deletion specially
Need to track parent while searching

questions.question: State one practical application where linked representation of binary trees is preferred and one where array representation is preferred. Justify your answer.
questions.hint: Think about what data structures use these representations in practice
keyPoints: Linked preferred: Expression tree parsing in compilers
Reason: tree shape varies, dynamic, frequent modifications
Array preferred: Binary heap for priority queue
Reason: complete binary tree property, fast access, no deletions in middle
File system: linked for dynamic hierarchy
Heap sort: array for efficiency

questions.question: A skewed binary tree (like a linked list) has n nodes. Calculate the number of NULL pointers in its linked representation and the number of empty positions in its array representation.
questions.hint: For a skewed tree of height n, array index for last node is n-1, but rightmost position needed is 2n-1
keyPoints: In linked representation: each node has 2 pointers
Total pointers = 2n
All nodes except one have exactly one NULL (the leaf has both NULL)
NULL pointers = n + 1 (n single NULLs + 1 leaf with 2)
Actually: n-1 nodes have children, so n+1 NULLs total
Array representation: tree has height n, so indices 0 to n-1 used
Array positions for a skewed tree of height n: need 2n+1 positions theoretically
Empty positions = (2n+1) - n = n+1
More space efficient: linked uses 2n pointers vs array needs 2n+1
